title: Parameter Typing
description: Lock down what goes INTO a function.

slides.title: The JS Problem 🐛
slides.content:
In pure JavaScript, you can't guarantee what data gets passed into your function. 

If you have a function `multiply(a, b)`, someone might accidentally call `multiply("five", 10)`, returning `NaN` (Not a Number).

slides.title: TypeScript to the Rescue 🦸
slides.content: With TypeScript, we annotate our parameters exactly like we annotate variables, using a colon `:`.

quizData.question: What happens if someone passes a string to a parameter strictly typed as `number`?

options.text: The function tries its best to convert the string to a number.
TypeScript catches the mismatch at compile-time and throws an error.
The app immediately crashes in the browser.

quizData.explanation: TypeScript acts as a bouncer, rejecting the compile process because the types don't match.

slides.title: Implicit 'Any' 🕵️
slides.content:
If you don't provide a type annotation for a parameter, TypeScript assumes it is of type `any`. 

This is usually a bad practice! Always type your parameters.

slides.title: Multiple Types
slides.content: Functions can take multiple parameters of vastly different types.
